ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for beancurd skin and ginkgo nut dessert soup; mapped to broader dessert food product class. A more specific term for Chinese sweet soup desserts is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soya bean curd products — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for beancurd skin (fuchok/yuba) component; ginkgo nut component not separately coded. Composite dish mapped at ingredient level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific composite code for beancurd skin and ginkgo nut dessert soup. A0CKW (soya bean curd products) used as the primary ingredient anchor. Composite sweet soup category not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Chinese dessert soup preparation. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for traditional Asian dessert dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beancurd skin and ginkgo nut dessert soup is approximately 250 g, typically served warm as a dessert or snack in Singaporean Chinese cuisine.

nutrient_source.title: Beancurd skin and gingko nuts dessert — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Beancurd skin and gingko nuts dessert' (cr_id F02050002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB entry are set to -1 (data not available).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beancurd skin and ginkgo nut dessert soup. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on analogous sweet soy-based dessert soups and the low carbohydrate content (4.04 g/100g). Beancurd skin (yuba/fuchok) is a minimally processed soy product with a low GI profile consistent with other soy foods (GI ~15–30 per University of Sydney GI Database). Rock sugar addition raises the effective GI modestly; the composite dish GI is estimated at 40 (low category). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (40 × 4.04 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.04 × 1.0 ≈ 4.0 g; per 100 g GL = (40 × 4.04) / 100 = 1.6. This is a conservative estimate; actual GI may vary with sugar quantity added.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (26 mg/100 g) and low sugar (3.02 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: With sodium at only 26 mg per 100 g, this dessert soup is well within safe limits for hypertensive individuals; however, patients should request reduced rock sugar versions to keep total sugar intake low and support blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low total carbohydrate (4.04 g/100 g), low sugar (3.02 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~40)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content and estimated low glycemic index make this dessert soup a relatively safe occasional choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided rock sugar is minimised or substituted with a low-GI sweetener and portion size is controlled to one standard bowl (250 g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.06 g/100 g), low sodium (26 mg/100 g), and soy isoflavones from beancurd skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low fat, low sodium profile and presence of soy isoflavones from beancurd skin support cardiovascular health; individuals with CVD should limit added rock sugar and consume ginkgo nuts in moderation (no more than 10 nuts per serving) to avoid potential adverse effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones and ginkgo flavonoids with antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beancurd skin provides soy isoflavones with mild anti-inflammatory properties and ginkgo nuts contain flavonoids and terpenoids with antioxidant activity; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise added sugar and limit ginkgo nut intake to moderate quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beancurd Skin and Ginkgo Nut Dessert Soup (腐竹白果糖水, fǔzhú báiguǒ tángshuǐ) is a traditional Chinese sweet dessert soup widely consumed in Singapore and Southern China. It is prepared by simmering dried beancurd skin sheets (fuchok or yuba — the protein-rich film formed on heated soy milk) together with shelled ginkgo nuts in water, sweetened with rock sugar. The dish is served warm or at room temperature as a dessert or afternoon snack. The texture is silky and soft from the rehydrated beancurd skin, with the ginkgo nuts providing a slightly chewy, mildly bitter counterpoint to the sweet broth. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (33 kcal/100 g), low in fat (1.06 g/100 g), and low in sodium (26 mg/100 g), making it one of the lighter traditional Chinese desserts. Protein content is modest at 1.69 g/100 g, contributed primarily by the beancurd skin. Calcium is notably present at 55 mg/100 g from the soy-derived beancurd skin. The estimated glycemic index is low (~40), and the glycemic load per standard 250 g bowl is approximately 4.0 g, making it suitable for most dietary patterns including those managing blood glucose. Clinically, the very low sodium and low fat profile support suitability for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. Ginkgo nuts should be consumed in moderation — no more than 10 per day — due to the presence of ginkgotoxin (4-O-methylpyridoxine), which can cause adverse neurological effects at high doses. The dish is plant-based, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-suitable.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F02050002) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence for GI component only) based on the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for soy-based dessert soups.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, most micronutrients, and moisture data for this traditional Singaporean Chinese dessert soup preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F02050002 as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central does not contain an exact match for this composite dish. 2. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate based on soy-based dessert soups and the low carbohydrate content of the dish; no direct GI measurement for this specific preparation was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 3. The input record used the spelling 'gingko' (variant); the canonical scientific spelling 'ginkgo' is used in this output. Both spellings are captured in aliases. 4. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been removed: with 4.04 g carbohydrate per 100 g and added rock sugar, this dish does not meet standard ketogenic dietary criteria. 5. Ginkgo nuts contain ginkgotoxin (4-O-methylpyridoxine); consumption should be limited to no more than 10 nuts per day to avoid potential neurological adverse effects.
